title: Living in the Crossfire: Favela Residents, Drug Dealers and Police Violence in Rio de Janeiro By Maria Helena Moreira Alves and Philip Evanson Temple University Press. 2011. 272 pages. $79.50 cloth, $33.95 paper
abstract: Living in the Crossfire: Favela Residents, Drug Dealers and Police Violence in Rio de Janeiro, by Maria Helena Moreira Alves and Philip Evanson (2011), provides a contemporary glance into the challenges facing favela dwellers (i.e., shantytown residents) and public security officials in the Brazilian city of Rio de Janeiro. As the authors note in their introduction, this book was produced in the wake of (and in response to) the militarized interventions by Rio's police forces during 2007 in the favela community of Complexo do Alemão. “During the period of siege,” observe Alves and Evanson, “significant numbers of people were killed or wounded who were not proved to have been involved in criminal activities.”(1) Though the police entered the community intent upon putting an end to drug trafficking activities there, they left many local residents terrified, increasing already existent feelings of fear and mistrust for municipal authorities. Through a series of interviews with favelados (favela residents) and state actors (e.g., public security officials, politicians, administrators), the aim of this book is to explore multiple sides of this ongoing struggle: as the authors write, their goal is “to contribute to the debate on human rights and public security in Brazil in the belief that there cannot be one without the other.”(7)
